steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: A large piece of wood floats on water while a small pebble sinks. This tells us…
options: The wood is lighter than the pebble so it floats
The wood has a lower density than water; the pebble has a higher density than water
Large objects always float and small objects always sink
The wood has more particles than the pebble
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Whether an object floats or sinks depends on its density compared to the fluid — not its mass or size. Wood has a lower density than water (it floats); the pebble has a higher density than water (it sinks).
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Floating and sinking depend on density, not mass. Wood is less dense than water; the pebble is more dense than water.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.3.1 — Density

steps.title: Density
steps.bodyHTML: Density is defined as the mass per unit volume of a substance and is calculated using ρ = m / V. Density depends on how closely the particles are packed and the mass of each particle — solids are usually denser than liquids, which are usually much denser than gases. Objects less dense than a liquid will float in it; objects more dense will sink.

relationships: Greater mass, same volume → higher density
Same mass, greater volume → lower density

commonErrors: Confusing density with mass — a large object is not necessarily denser
Using cm³ for volume without converting to m³ (1 m³ = 1 000 000 cm³)
Forgetting to include the unit for density (kg/m³ or g/cm³)

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Drag the sliders to change the mass and volume of an object. Watch how the density changes.

aqa.questionText: A block of metal has a mass of 540 g and a volume of 200 cm³. Calculate its density in kg/m³.

steps.label: Convert units
steps.content: Convert mass to kg and volume to m³:

steps.label: Substitute and calculate
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — correct substitution; 1 mark — correct answer with unit

aqa.questionText: A cube of plastic has a mass of 80 g and a volume of 100 cm³. What is its density in g/cm³?
aqa.markSchemeAward: 1 mark equation · 1 mark substitution · 1 mark correct answer + unit
aqa.commonMistake: Option C results from dividing volume by mass instead of mass by volume.

equation.examTip: Watch your units — convert cm³ to m³ by dividing by 1 000 000 (10⁶). Convert g to kg by dividing by 1000.

boardNotes.aqa: On AQA formula sheet. Must know all three rearrangements and unit conversions.
